name: Amnioinfusion for meconium-stained amniotic fluid
summary: Across 23 randomized trials (5994 participants), prophylactic intrapartum amnioinfusion for meconium-stained amniotic fluid roughly halved meconium aspiration syndrome (MAS) and also reduced meconium below the cords, low Apgar scores, neonatal acidosis, cesarean delivery, and NICU admission; postpartum endometritis was unchanged. The benefit is concentrated in high-risk, limited-surveillance settings.
description: A meta-analysis (Davis 2023, AJOG) of 24 RCTs of prophylactic transcervical amnioinfusion in labor complicated by moderate-to-thick MSAF. The primary outcome (MAS) is entered here as each trial’s raw per-arm event counts (Supplemental Figure 4) and pooled live; the seven secondary outcomes are entered as the review’s per-trial odds ratios (Supplemental Figures 5–11). The review excluded Sadovsky 1989 from the MAS analysis, so 23 trials contribute to MAS.
interpretation: The pooled MAS effect is statistically robust but its absolute size does not travel. Pooled control MAS risk here is ~9.5% and treated ~3.7% (absolute reduction ~5.8 points), which works out to a number-needed-to-treat of roughly 17. But that is an NNT for an enriched, high-risk group—women already in labor with moderate-to-thick MSAF—not for all laboring patients, all MSAF, post-term pregnancy, or induction. MSAF complicates about 1 in 7 pregnancies while MAS occurs in up to ~5% of MSAF infants, so the ~10% pooled control risk is itself high: it reflects trials from 1989–2017 with heterogeneous MAS definitions, variable monitoring and neonatal care, differing meconium thresholds, substantial between-study heterogeneity (I² 56%), and acknowledged publication bias (Egger P=.02). In a selected population with a baseline MAS risk near 10% this meta-analysis estimates NNT ≈17 (GRADE: “6 fewer per 100,” implied range ~14–25); that baseline is not portable to contemporary routine obstetrics, so “NNT 17” should not be repeated as a general clinical number. The review’s own 95% prediction interval for MAS was 0.07–1.55—in a plausible future setting the true effect could range from large benefit to none or harm. Benefit was larger with limited peripartum surveillance and in developing-country settings, while the large, well-monitored Fraser 2005 trial found no MAS benefit (OR 1.41), which drives the heterogeneity.
methodologyNotes: MAS is entered as raw per-arm event counts (Supplemental Figure 4) so the engine computes the risk ratio, pooled control risk, and NNT directly from the data—nothing hardcoded; our pooled control rate (292/3078) and treated rate (109/2916) match the review, and the review’s pooled odds ratio was 0.33 (95% CI 0.21–0.51). Because MAS is uncommon the risk ratio approximates that odds ratio. The seven secondary outcomes are entered as the review’s published per-trial odds ratios (Supplemental Figures 5–11) and pooled live by random-effects (DerSimonian–Laird) on the log-odds scale. Sadovsky 1989 is omitted from MAS and from Apgar (the review marked it excluded there); Yellayi is omitted from Apgar for the same reason; each secondary outcome includes exactly the trials the review plotted. The review’s per-trial CIs are log-symmetric Wald intervals (lower = point²/upper), so where a lower bound prints as 0.00 it is entered as that implied bound rather than an unusable zero. The review’s forest plots label three trials differently from its reference list (‘Gupta 2016’ is Prachi et al, ‘Rathore 2002’ is Rathor et al, and figure years for Choudhary and Elsersy differ from the published 2010 and 2017); citations and years here follow the published references. The review reported significant small-study/publication-bias signals for MAS, meconium below the cords, cesarean delivery, and neonatal acidosis.

outcomes.label: Meconium aspiration syndrome
outcomes.description: Neonatal meconium aspiration syndrome as defined in each trial (clinical, CXR-based, or oxygen/ventilation criteria varied). Entered as raw per-arm event counts from the review’s Supplemental Figure 4.

outcomes.label: Meconium below the cords
outcomes.description: Meconium visualized below the vocal cords at birth.

outcomes.label: Cesarean delivery for fetal distress
outcomes.description: Cesarean section performed for nonreassuring fetal heart tracing / fetal distress.

outcomes.label: NICU admission
outcomes.description: Admission to the neonatal intensive care unit.

outcomes.label: Postpartum endometritis
outcomes.description: Maternal postpartum endometritis (the one outcome not significantly reduced).

studies.author: Adam
studies.citation: Adam K, Cano L, Moise KJ. The effect of intrapartum amnioinfusion on the outcome of the fetus with heavy meconium stained amniotic fluid. Proceedings of 9th Annual Meeting of the Society of Perinatal Obstetricians; 1989; New Orleans, LA. 1989:438.
studies.doseRegimen: Single infusion of 1000 mL

studies.author: Bhosale
studies.citation: Bhosale R, Patil S, Patil Y, Kshirsagar N. Randomised clinical trial of amnioinfusion and caesarean section in cases of thick meconium stained amniotic fluid and neonatal outcome. Int J Recent Trends Sci Technol 2015;13:511-5.
studies.doseRegimen: Normal saline

studies.author: Choudhary
studies.citation: Choudhary D, Bano I, Ali SM. Does amnioinfusion reduce caesarean section rate in meconium-stained amniotic fluid? Arch Gynecol Obstet 2010;282:17-22.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 500 mL over 30 min, then 2 mL/min
studies.notes: Modified Jadad 6. Figure labels this trial '2009'.
studies.endpointDefinition: CXR and clinical course consistent with MAS.

studies.author: Cialone
studies.citation: Cialone PR, Sherer DM, Ryan RM, Sinkin RA, Abramovici JS. Amnioinfusion during labor complicated by particulate meconium stained amniotic fluid decreases neonatal morbidity. Am J Obstet Gynecol 1994;170:842-9.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 600 mL over >1 h, then 150 mL/h
studies.endpointDefinition: MAS categories (subclinical, mild, or medium) based on O2 requirement and duration.

studies.author: Elsersy
studies.citation: Elsersy M. Utility of amnioinfusion in deliveries complicated by meconium stained liquor: a randomized controlled trial. Int J Reprod Contracept Obstet Gynecol 2017;6:65-71.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 500 mL over 30 min, then 300 mL at 3 mL/min
studies.notes: Modified Jadad 5. Figure labels this trial '2016'.
studies.endpointDefinition: RDS within a few hours of birth with compatible CXR finding.

studies.author: Eriksen
studies.citation: Eriksen NL, Hostetter M, Parisi VM. Prophylactic amnioinfusion in pregnancies complicated by thick meconium. Am J Obstet Gynecol 1994;171:1026-30.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 800 mL over >1 h, then 180 mL/h

studies.author: Fonseca
studies.citation: Fonseca A, Dwivedi A. A randomised single blind control study to assess the effect of intrapartum amnioinfusion on maternal caesarean section rate and fetal outcome in a tertiary care centre. Int J Reprod Contracept Obstet Gynecol 2017;6:4347-50.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 500 mL over 30 min

studies.author: Fraser
studies.citation: Fraser WD, Hofmeyr J, Lede R, et al. Amnioinfusion for the prevention of the meconium aspiration syndrome. N Engl J Med 2005;353:909-17.
studies.doseRegimen: 800 mL saline at 20 mL/min over 40 min
studies.notes: Modified Jadad 7. Largest trial; continuous monitoring and standardized care.
studies.endpointDefinition: MAS adjudicated by a panel of 3 blinded neonatologists.

studies.author: Gavali
studies.citation: Gavali, Shinde S, Aher G, Mhaske S. Study of role of intrapartum transcervical amnioinfusion in meconium stained amniotic fluid. Indian J Basic Appl Med Res 2017;6:389-97.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 250 mL over 20-30 min, then 10-20 mL/min up to 600 mL

studies.author: Prachi
studies.citation: Prachi, Reena P, Priya BK, Swati T, Ruchi S, Sarita M. Study of perinatal outcome of amnio infusion during labor in meconium stained amniotic fluid. Scholars J Appl Sci Med 2016;4:2048-51.
studies.doseRegimen: 600 mL sterile saline over 30 min, then fluid until liquor cleared (max 1 L)
studies.notes: Modified Jadad 3. Labeled 'Gupta 2016' in the review's forest plots.

studies.author: Hofmeyr
studies.citation: Hofmeyr GJ, Gulmezoglu AM, Buchmann E, et al. The collaborative randomised amnioinfusion for meconium project (CRAMP): 1. South Africa. Br J Obstet Gynaecol 1998;105:304-8.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 800 mL at 15 mL/min, then 3 mL/min maintenance
studies.endpointDefinition: Diagnosed clinically by a blinded pediatrician.

studies.author: Ilagan
studies.citation: Ilagan MKN, Kazzi GS, Liang K, Womack S, Bronsteen R. Transcervical amnioinfusion for prevention of meconium aspiration. Pediatr Res 1992;31.
studies.doseRegimen: 500 mL of NS

studies.author: Macri
studies.citation: Macri CJ, Schrimmer DB, Leung A, Greenspoon JS, Paul RH. Prophylactic amnioinfusion improves outcome of pregnancy complicated by thick meconium and oligohydramnios. Am J Obstet Gynecol 1992;167:117-21.
studies.doseRegimen: Warmed saline 500 mL, then 250-500 mL to keep AFI >10 cm

studies.author: Mahomed
studies.citation: Mahomed K, Mulambo T, Woelk G, Hofmeyr GJ, Gulmezoglu AM. The collaborative randomised amnioinfusion for meconium project (CRAMP): 2. Zimbabwe. Br J Obstet Gynaecol 1998;105:309-13.
studies.doseRegimen: NS 500 mL over 30 min, then 500 mL at 30 drops/min

studies.author: Moodley
studies.citation: Moodley J, Matchaba P, Payne AJ. Intrapartum amnioinfusion for meconium-stained liquor in developing countries. Trop Doct 1998;28:31-4.

studies.author: Puertas
studies.citation: Puertas A, Paz Carrillo M, Molto L, Alvarez M, Sedeno S, Miranda JA. Meconium-stained amniotic fluid in labor: a randomized trial of prophylactic amnioinfusion. Eur J Obstet Gynecol Reprod Biol 2001;99:33-7.

studies.notes: Modified Jadad 5. Labeled 'Rathore 2002' in the review's forest plots.
studies.endpointDefinition: RDS within a few hours of birth with compatible CXR finding.
